hints.to speak: That would be 'loqui' — 'Conticuere' comes from 'conticescere' (to fall silent)
hints.to gather: That would be 'colligere' — 'Conticuere' comes from 'conticescere' (to fall silent)
hints.to tremble: That would be 'tremere' — 'Conticuere' comes from 'conticescere' (to fall silent)

steps.reveal: Conticuere = they fell silent (syncopated form of conticuerunt)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'conticescunt' — 'Conticuere' is a syncopated perfect (= conticuerunt)
hints.imperfect indicative: Imperfect would be 'conticescebant' — 'Conticuere' is perfect indicative
hints.pluperfect indicative: Pluperfect would be 'conticuerant' — 'Conticuere' is perfect indicative

hints.taceo, tacere, tacui, tacitum: These are the parts of 'tacere' (to be silent) — 'conticescere' is the inchoative compound
hints.contingo, contingere, contigi, contactum: These are the parts of 'contingere' (to touch/reach)
hints.contineo, continere, continui, contentum: These are the parts of 'continere' (to hold together)

hints.accusative: While 'omnes' could be accusative plural, here it is the nominative subject of 'conticuere'
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'omnibus' — 'omnes' is nominative here
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'omnium' — 'omnes' is nominative, subject of 'conticuere'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nominative plural ends in -ae — 'omnes' ends in -es (3rd declension adj.)
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension nominative plural ends in -i — 'omnes' ends in -es
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nominative plural ends in -us — 'omnes' ends in -es

hints.nominative: While 'ora' could be nominative plural neuter, here it is the accusative object of 'tenebant'
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'oribus' — 'ora' is accusative plural
hints.vocative: Vocative would match nominative — but 'ora' here is accusative, object of 'tenebant'

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'oris' ends in -is (3rd declension)
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'oris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'oris' ends in -is

hints.to stretch: That would be 'tendere' — 'tenebant' comes from 'tenere' (to hold)
hints.to fear: That would be 'timere' — 'tenebant' comes from 'tenere' (to hold)
hints.to try: That would be 'temptare' — 'tenebant' comes from 'tenere' (to hold)

steps.reveal: tenebant = they were holding (i.e., they held their faces intent)

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'tenent' — the -ba- infix marks the imperfect
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'tenuerunt' — 'tenebant' with -ba- is imperfect
hints.imperfect subjunctive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'tenerent' — 'tenebant' is imperfect indicative

hints.tendo, tendere, tetendi, tentum: These are the parts of 'tendere' (to stretch) — note the different conjugation
hints.tempto, temptare, temptavi, temptatum: These are the parts of 'temptare' (to try/test)
hints.timeo, timere, timui, —: These are the parts of 'timere' (to fear)

translation: All fell silent and held their faces intent
